summary: Peter Woit critiques two AMS Notices articles by NSA officials that appear to mislead the mathematics community about the NSA's role in backdooring the DUAL_EC_DRBG cryptography standard. The first article by Richard George claimed no proven NSA-linked cryptographic weaknesses exist, while a follow-up by NSA Director of Research Michael Wertheimer expressed 'regret' without actually denying the backdoor's existence. Woit notes that the core mathematical question—how were the elliptic curve points P and Q chosen—remains deliberately unanswered, with the NSA refusing to cooperate with investigations. He argues the AMS leadership must demand explicit assurances from the NSA or sever cooperative programs, as the organization is being used to spread disinformation to mathematicians.
key_points: The NSA has published two articles in AMS Notices that appear to deny backdooring crypto standards while carefully avoiding explicit statements about the specific DUAL_EC_DRBG case
The mathematical question is simple and verifiable: how were points P and Q chosen? The NSA refuses to answer and told NIST staff they couldn't discuss it publicly
Wertheimer's 'regrettable' statement expresses hindsight about not dropping support after the backdoor potential was discovered, but never denies the backdoor exists
Security expert Matthew Green confirms the articles are misleading and believes NSA policy of tampering with cryptographic products continues
Woit presents two stark possibilities: either there's no backdoor and NSA inexplicably won't say so, or the AMS is being used to mislead mathematicians
money_quotes: I have never heard of any proven weakness in a cryptographic algorithm that's linked to NSA; just innuendo.
With hindsight, NSA should have ceased supporting the dual EC_DRBG algorithm immediately after security researchers discovered the potential for a trapdoor.
In truth, I can think of no better way to describe our failure to drop support for the Dual_EC_DRBG algorithm as anything other than regrettable.

The only record of what happened when the NIST asked about how P and Q were chosen early on in the development of the standard indicates that people were told by the NSA that they were not allowed to publicly discuss the question.
I think that the NSA has really adopted a policy of tampering with cryptographic products and they're not going to give that up.
I don't think there can be any argument about the significance of the AMS being used by the NSA to mislead the mathematics community.
key_insight: When officials express 'regret' about a controversy without explicitly denying the underlying accusation, their careful word choices often reveal more than their statements conceal.
